content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array AAA of size NNN.

On standing at the ithi^{th}ith index of the array (1≤i≤N)(1\le i \le N)(1≤i≤N), Chef can make a jump of length **less than or equal to** AiA_iAi​.

If Chef starts from the **first** index of the array, find the **minimum** number of jumps required by Chef to reach the **last** index of the array.

If it is impossible to reach the last index, print −1-1−1 instead.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number elements in the array.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers - the elements of the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the **minimum** number of jumps required by Chef to reach the **last** element of the array.


If it is impossible to reach the last index, print −1-1−1 instead.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains an integer N, the size of the array.
- The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
Indices are 1‑based. Chef starts at index 1 and wants to reach index N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of jumps required to reach the last index. If it is impossible, output -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Minimum number of moves required to reach the last index is 333:

Move 111: Make a jump of length 1≤A11 \le A_11≤A1​. Thus, Chef moves from index 111 to index 222.
Move 222: Make a jump of length 1≤A21 \le A_21≤A2​. Thus, Chef moves from index 222 to index 333.
Move 333: Make a jump of length 1≤A31 \le A_31≤A3​. Thus, Chef moves from index 333 to index 444.

Test case 222: Minimum number of moves required to reach the last index is 222:

Move 111: Make a jump of length 1≤A11 \le A_11≤A1​. Thus, Chef moves from index 111 to index 222.
Move 222: Make a jump of length 2≤A22 \le A_22≤A2​. Thus, Chef moves from index 222 to index 444.

Test case 333: It is not possible to reach the last index using any number of moves.
Test case 444: It is not possible to reach the last index using any number of moves.

solution.approach:
The classic "minimum jumps" problem can be solved in O(N) using a greedy scan. While traversing the array we keep:
- `farthest`: the farthest index reachable from any position within the current jump range.
- `currentEnd`: the rightmost index that can be reached with the current number of jumps.
- `jumps`: the number of jumps performed so far.
Iterate i from 1 to N‑1 (1‑based). At each i update `farthest = max(farthest, i + A[i])`. When i reaches `currentEnd` we must increase the jump count because we have exhausted the current range; set `currentEnd = farthest`. If at any point `currentEnd` does not move forward (i.e., `farthest` ≤ i) and we are not yet at the last index, the destination is unreachable. Edge cases: if N == 1 answer is 0; if the first element is 0 and N > 1 answer is -1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the array. The total over all test cases is O(ΣN) ≤ O(2·10^5).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input array.
